name: Rapid AF
identifyBy: Atrial fibrillation with ventricular rate >110 bpm
Irregularly irregular rhythm, absent P waves
May cause haemodynamic compromise at rates >150 bpm
Common precipitants: infection, pain, hyperthyroidism, PE, decompensated heart failure
management: IV rate control: Metoprolol 5mg IV or Diltiazem 10–20mg IV
If haemodynamically compromised: synchronised DC cardioversion
Caution with rate control in heart failure - avoid negatively inotropic agents
pearls: 'Rapid AF' is not a separate rhythm - it's AF with inadequate rate control
Rate >150: consider AF with 2:1 flutter - look closely for flutter waves
Target resting ventricular rate <80–110 bpm depending on guidelines and patient
Avoid verapamil in decompensated HF - can precipitate acute decompensation
Digoxin: slow rate control, mainly effective at rest - poor for exercise-induced tachycardia
differentials: AF with good rate control (same rhythm, different rate)
MAT (distinct P waves, usually in COPD context)
